title: The Crimson Kiss of Two Giants: Helium Detonation and High-Energy Neutrino Production
summary_bullets: This paper introduces "erythrohenosis"—the coalescence of degenerate helium cores during red-giant collisions—as a novel class of transient that can produce high-energy (TeV–PeV) neutrinos, and builds a semi-analytic model anchored to MESA stellar models and 3D SPH simulations. It estimates a gravitational energy release ≈4.28×10^49 erg that heats the remnant to Tf≈5.3×10^8 K and, via small-scale dynamo action, can amplify magnetic fields to order B≈1.8×10^10 G, enabling hadronic acceleration in magnetized outflows and a neutrino flux that could explain part of IceCube’s diffuse signal and be detectable from a single event within ≲2 Mpc. The authors also quantify thermal and nuclear (MeV) neutrino channels (notably 18F decay) and argue that thermal neutrino losses are negligible on dynamical timescales, proposing a unique multimessenger signature of neutrinos + gravitational waves + possible electromagnetic emission from these collisions.

Why Yanbei would care: the paper connects dynamical, nonlinear merger physics to observable multimessenger signals and provides semi-quantitative estimates (energetics, timescales, magnetic saturation, particle acceleration) that can be used to predict transient gravitational-wave burst properties and neutrino counterparts; this matches Yanbei’s interests in waveform modeling of transients, strong-field observables, multimessenger probes, and the astrophysical populations that set event rates. Moreover, the work’s focus on rapid nonlinear evolution, magnetic-field amplification, and energy partitioning speaks to his interest in nonlinear gravitational-wave interactions and how astrophysical microphysics maps onto detectable signatures and tests of fundamental physics.

Keywords/topics: red-giant core collisions, helium detonation (erythrohenosis), TeV–PeV neutrinos, MESA + 3D SPH, magnetic dynamo / B-field saturation, hadronic particle acceleration, nuclear (18F) neutrinos, multimessenger (neutrinos + GW + EM). Published on arXiv in late March 2026 (arXiv:2603.22419; PDF dated March 25, 2026) — very recent.
